title: Quiz: Probability Distributions
description: Test your understanding of probability distributions in quantum computing

questions.question: A qubit has P(1) = 0.4. If we measure it 100 times, what is the expected number of 1s?
What is the standard deviation of the number of 1s when measuring a qubit with P(1)=0.5 for 400 shots?
To halve the standard error in probability estimation, how many times more shots do you need?
questions.explanation: Standard error scales as 1/√n. To halve it, you need √n to double, so n must quadruple (4×).

questions.question: The Gaussian distribution is a good approximation for the Binomial distribution when n is large.
questions.explanation: True! By the Central Limit Theorem, Binomial(n,p) ≈ N(np, np(1-p)) for large n.

questions.question: A qubit has decoherence time T₂ = 50 μs. What is the probability it survives for 100 μs?
To encode the probability distribution [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25] in a quantum state, what should the amplitudes be?
questions.explanation: Amplitudes are √p. For p=0.25, amplitude = √0.25 = 0.5 for each basis state.

questions.question: The exponential distribution has the 'memoryless' property: knowing you've waited time s doesn't change the distribution of remaining wait time.
questions.explanation: True! This is a defining property of the exponential distribution: P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describe shot noise? (Select all that apply)

options.text: It decreases as the number of measurements increases
It can be completely eliminated with enough shots

questions.explanation: a), b), c) are all correct. d) is false - shot noise always exists for finite n, though it can be made arbitrarily small.
